title: Paper Reproduction Requirements
description: Structured requirements extracted from an ML/AI research paper, used to plan and execute a faithful or scaled reproduction.

paper_id.description: Unique identifier for the paper, typically an arXiv ID (e.g. '2301.12345') or DOI.

method_name.description: Name of the method, model, or system proposed in the paper (e.g. 'FlashAttention-2', 'DPO', 'Mamba').

core_claim.description: The main empirical claim the paper argues it demonstrates. Must be a single falsifiable statement. Example: 'DPO matches or exceeds PPO-based RLHF on summarization quality while being simpler to implement and 2x faster to train.'

datasets.description: All datasets used in the paper's experiments, including pretraining data, fine-tuning data, and evaluation benchmarks.

name.description: Dataset name as referred to in the paper (e.g. 'OpenWebText', 'TL;DR', 'MMLU').

size.description: Dataset size as stated in the paper (e.g. '9B tokens', '120k examples', '14M images'). Null if not stated.

source.description: URL or description of where to obtain the dataset (e.g. 'https://huggingface.co/datasets/openwebtext', 'available upon request from authors'). Null if not stated.

preprocessing.description: Preprocessing steps described in the paper (e.g. 'BPE tokenization with GPT-2 tokenizer, max sequence length 1024, deduplication via MinHash'). Null if not described.

architecture.description: Architecture name and key structural details (e.g. 'Transformer decoder-only, 12 layers, 12 heads, 768 hidden dim, pre-norm with LayerNorm, GELU activation').

parameters.description: Total parameter count as stated (e.g. '125M', '1.3B'). Null if not stated.

key_hyperparameters.description: Named hyperparameters that define the model structure. Keys are parameter names, values are their settings. Include ALL stated values: n_layers, n_heads, d_model, d_ff, dropout, activation, normalization, positional_encoding, vocab_size, max_seq_len, tie_embeddings, etc.

training.description: Training configuration. Every field defaults to null when not stated in the paper. Do not guess values.

optimizer.description: Optimizer name and any non-default settings (e.g. 'AdamW with beta1=0.9, beta2=0.95, eps=1e-8').

learning_rate.description: Peak learning rate as a float (e.g. 0.0003 for 3e-4).

lr_schedule.description: Learning rate schedule details (e.g. 'cosine decay to 1e-5 over full training, with 2000 step linear warmup').

batch_size.description: Effective batch size (accounting for gradient accumulation and data parallelism). If the paper gives per-GPU batch size, multiply out.

gradient_accumulation_steps.description: Number of gradient accumulation steps if stated.

epochs_or_steps.description: Training duration as stated (e.g. '3 epochs', '100k steps', '300B tokens').

warmup.description: Warmup configuration (e.g. '2000 steps linear warmup', '1% of total steps').

data_augmentation.description: Any data augmentation techniques described (e.g. 'random cropping to 224x224, horizontal flip, color jitter').

mixed_precision.description: Mixed precision strategy (e.g. 'bf16 with fp32 master weights', 'fp16 with loss scaling').

hardware.description: Hardware the authors used (e.g. '8x A100 80GB with NVLink', '64 TPUv4 chips').

estimated_gpu_hours.description: Estimated total GPU/TPU hours if stated or directly calculable (e.g. '256 A100-hours'). Do not estimate if not clearly inferable.

metrics.description: All quantitative results claimed in the paper that could be reproduced.

name.description: Metric name exactly as used in the paper (e.g. 'perplexity', 'BLEU-4', 'top-1 accuracy', 'win rate').

claimed_value.description: The specific numerical value claimed. Use a number when possible (e.g. 29.1). Use a string for ranges or qualitative claims (e.g. '>90%', '~3x speedup'). Null if not directly stated as a number.

dataset.description: Which dataset or split this metric was measured on (e.g. 'WikiText-103 test', 'MMLU 5-shot', 'ImageNet val').

higher_is_better.description: True if higher values indicate better performance (accuracy, BLEU). False if lower is better (perplexity, loss, FID).

table_or_figure.description: Where in the paper this value appears (e.g. 'Table 2 row 3', 'Figure 4 blue line at x=100k').

conditions.description: Any conditions for this measurement (e.g. 'zero-shot', '5-shot', 'with retrieval augmentation', 'ensemble of 5').

is_main_claim.description: True ONLY for the 1-5 metrics most prominently claimed in the paper's abstract or introduction. These are the results the paper's core argument rests on. All other metrics (ablations, supplementary experiments, baselines) are false.

ablations.description: Ablation studies reported in the paper. These help determine which components are critical vs. optional.

description.description: What this ablation tests (e.g. 'Compares flash attention vs. standard attention vs. linear attention, showing flash attention gives 2x speedup with no quality loss').

released_artifacts.description: Pre-trained checkpoints, datasets, and evaluation scripts released by the authors. Critical for determining reproduction strategy — evaluation-only is 1-2 orders of magnitude cheaper than training.

what_it_is.description: What this checkpoint contains, e.g. 'trained LoRA adapter for GPT-2 Medium on E2E NLG'

supports_main_claim.description: True if evaluating this checkpoint directly tests one of the is_main_claim metrics

code_url.description: URL to the official code repository if found in the paper, footnotes, abstract, or metadata. Null if none found.

reproducibility_notes.description: Any statements the authors make about reproducibility (e.g. 'Code and checkpoints at github.com/...', 'See Appendix F for full hyperparameters', 'Random seeds: 0, 1, 2'). Null if none.

compute_feasibility_note.description: 1-2 sentences. Identify the cheapest reproduction strategy that tests the paper's main claim. If a released checkpoint exists for the main-claim experiment, evaluation-only is typically CPU-feasible regardless of model size. State this strategy and rough memory/time estimate. Only mark a paper infeasible if even evaluation-only requires GPU/multi-machine resources.
